title: What Comes After The Refugee Camp | NOEMA
gist: The global humanitarian refugee regime built since the 1950s is collapsing as the US (USAID), EU, UK, France and Germany slash aid, leaving 6.6 million camp residents stranded. As funding-based 'care' disappears, the refugee camp is being replaced by border-externalization and detention-center models, just as climate-driven displacement is projected to quadruple by 2050.

insights.claim: WFP cut monthly per-refugee assistance at Kakuma from $17 (2023) to $13, then to $5 in 2025, after Trump suspended US foreign assistance and shuttered USAID, stranding 100,000+ residents.
insights.speaker: JOSHUA CRAZE
insights.so_what: Aid-dependent supply chains and NGO contractors face structural revenue collapse; watch WFP/UNHCR vendor and logistics exposure in the Horn of Africa.
insights.locator: After U.S. President Donald Trump abruptly suspended

insights.claim: UNHCR laid off 30% of its workforce and cut assistance to 13 million refugees, while IOM let 6,000 staff go as its budget fell 25% in 2025 and contracted further in 2026.
insights.speaker: JOSHUA CRAZE
insights.so_what: The multilateral institutions underpinning displacement response are shrinking fast; counterparty and continuity risk for any program reliant on UN agency delivery.

insights.claim: Global North aid retreat is structural, not just Trump-driven: the EU, France, Germany cut humanitarian funding and the UK slashed foreign assistance in 2025-26 proportionately even more aggressively than the US.
insights.speaker: JOSHUA CRAZE
insights.so_what: Do not treat aid cuts as a reversible US policy quirk; the entire donor base is in secular retreat, signaling a durable shift in capital flows away from humanitarianism.

themes.theme: Border externalization and the detention-center economy

insights.claim: Capital is shifting from feeding the displaced to 'migration management': border enforcement consumes vastly more spending than aid, and the detention center had replaced the refugee camp as a policy priority even before Trump's cuts.
insights.speaker: JOSHUA CRAZE
insights.so_what: Budget is migrating from humanitarian NGOs toward border-security, surveillance, drone and detention contractors; follow the enforcement money rather than aid budgets.
insights.quote: Even before Trump's cuts, the detention center had replaced the refugee camp as a policy priority.

insights.claim: Europe externalized its frontiers via paid deals, including a $6.6 billion EU-Turkey deal (2016) and EU-funded militias, surveillance and detention in Niger, Libya, Sudan, Tunisia, and now expanding deportation agreements with Senegal and Mauritania.
insights.speaker: JOSHUA CRAZE
insights.so_what: Sustained EU outlays to third-country security forces create a recurring contract pipeline; geopolitical risk concentrates in transit states paid to be Europe's border.
insights.locator: As the Syrian refugee crisis worsened

insights.claim: Border hardening reroutes rather than reduces migration: externalizing into North Africa surged the Senegal-Canary Islands sea route, and hardening Morocco's borders skyrocketed departures along the Atlantic route from Mauritania.
insights.speaker: JOSHUA CRAZE
insights.so_what: Enforcement spending displaces flows rather than stopping them, implying perpetual escalation; the detention model offers no terminal solution and locks in recurring spend.
insights.locator: As popular as these policies may be

insights.claim: Migration into Europe is driven by geopolitical conditions (wars, catastrophes) rather than EU border policy, so barbed wire and drones cannot change the underlying number of displaced people.
insights.speaker: JOSHUA CRAZE
insights.so_what: Bets premised on border tech 'solving' migration misread the driver; political volatility in source regions remains the dominant variable to watch.
insights.locator: All these schemes ignore the fact

themes.theme: Climate displacement as the next structural shock

insights.claim: The number of people displaced by climate catastrophe is projected to quadruple by 2050, yet climate refugees are not granted refugee status under the 1951 UN Refugee Convention and the 1967 Protocol.
insights.speaker: JOSHUA CRAZE
insights.so_what: A massive, legally unrecognized displacement wave is building with no institutional framework to absorb it; a long-horizon macro and geopolitical destabilizer.
insights.quote: Globally, the number of people displaced by climate catastrophe is projected to quadruple by 2050.

insights.claim: By end-2025, 117 million people were displaced worldwide (down slightly from a record 123 million in 2024), versus 38 million in 2000, with Sudan the largest displacement crisis alongside DRC, Myanmar and Syria.
insights.speaker: JOSHUA CRAZE
insights.so_what: Displacement has roughly tripled since 2000 and remains near record highs; a structurally rising baseline that border and aid systems are failing to absorb.
insights.locator: The crisis in the global refugee regime

themes.theme: Failure of 'camp as city' economic-zone schemes

insights.claim: The Jordan Compact (2016) failed at every level despite $1.4 billion World Bank credit and ~$4 billion in refugee aid: only 11 companies joined within two years, refugees were restricted to low-wage menial labor, and investors stayed uninterested.
insights.speaker: JOSHUA CRAZE
insights.so_what: The flagship 'put refugees to work in special economic zones' model has a track record of failure; discount optimistic 'refugee-as-labor' development theses.
insights.locator: One scheme that warns against optimistic readings

insights.claim: Kenya's Shirika Plan will redesignate Dadaab and Kakuma as municipalities at a cost of around a billion dollars (largely World Bank funded), but arid landscapes, aid cuts and absent political/work rights make their economic viability doubtful.
insights.speaker: JOSHUA CRAZE
insights.so_what: A billion-dollar World Bank-backed 'camp-to-city' bet rests on shaky agronomic and legal foundations; high execution risk for the 'cities of the future' narrative.

insights.claim: Kakuma's local economy was about $56 million before Trump's cuts, illustrating that camp economies are real but fragile and heavily aid-dependent.
insights.speaker: JOSHUA CRAZE
insights.so_what: Camp-based commerce represents a measurable but aid-contingent market; demand collapses when rations are cut, undermining self-sustaining-economy claims.
insights.locator: Kakuma, at least before the economic crisis

themes.theme: Political economy of the refugee regime

insights.claim: UNHCR's three durable solutions (return, resettlement, integration) are all now politically unviable, leaving the agency functioning as a proxy for donor states that fund the Libyan coast guard and police detention rather than enabling refugee movement.
insights.speaker: JOSHUA CRAZE
insights.so_what: The multilateral refugee architecture has inverted its original mandate; its political capture by donor enforcement priorities is now the operative reality for any partner.
insights.locator: Today's UNHCR is in an unenviable position

insights.claim: Far-right anti-asylum politics are now electorally dominant: Germany's AfD won nearly 21% in the February 2025 election, and Chancellor Merz urged Syrian refugees to return in March 2026 with threats of deportation.
insights.speaker: JOSHUA CRAZE
insights.so_what: Restrictive migration policy is a structural vote-winner across Europe; expect a durable rightward, anti-asylum policy ratchet regardless of which parties govern.
insights.locator: The rightward tilt of global politics

insights.claim: The new European Pact on Migration and Asylum took effect June 12, detaining irregular arrivals and pushing them into accelerated procedures without legal-aid safeguards, effectively criminalizing asylum.
insights.speaker: JOSHUA CRAZE
entities: European Pact on Migration and Asylum
insights.so_what: A binding EU-wide legal shift toward detention-first processing signals expanding detention infrastructure demand and durable hostility to inbound asylum.
insights.locator: On June 12, a new European Pact
